questions.prompt: Next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative sacrificed when a choice is made — it includes both explicit (out-of-pocket) and implicit (forgone) costs. On the PPC, the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of Good X is the units of Good Y given up. AP FRQ trap: economists always include implicit costs, so a firm earning accounting profit may still earn zero or negative economic profit.

questions.prompt: Condition where wants exceed available resources.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the fundamental economic problem: human wants are unlimited but the resources (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship) needed to satisfy them are finite. Scarcity forces every economic agent — individuals, firms, governments — to make choices, which generate opportunity costs and trade-offs. Without scarcity there would be no need for economics.

questions.prompt: Principle that producing more of one good usually requires giving up increasing amounts of another.
questions.explanation: As production of one good increases along the PPC, ever-larger amounts of the other good must be sacrificed, because resources are not equally suited to all uses. This bows the PPC outward (concave to the origin). If opportunity costs were constant, the PPC would be a straight line. The law underlies comparative advantage and gains from specialization.

questions.prompt: Choices that require giving up one option, benefit, or goal to get another.
questions.explanation: A trade-off is a situation where getting more of one thing means accepting less of another due to scarcity. Every point on the PPC captures a trade-off between two goods; moving along the curve requires sacrificing some of one good to gain more of the other. Recognizing trade-offs is the foundation of economic reasoning — resources are finite, wants are unlimited.

questions.prompt: Model showing scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, and trade-offs.
questions.explanation: The PPC shows the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when all resources are fully and efficiently employed. Points on the curve are efficient; inside = inefficient (waste); outside = unattainable now. Outward shifts signal economic growth (more resources or better technology). The slope measures opportunity cost; the concave shape reflects the law of increasing opportunity cost.

questions.prompt: Frontier model showing trade-offs and efficient combinations under resource limits.
questions.explanation: With trade, a country can consume combinations of goods beyond its own PPC by exporting its comparative-advantage good and importing the other. The consumption possibilities frontier lies outside the PPC along the export good axis, showing how specialization + trade raises living standards. Its slope equals the terms of trade (relative price of traded goods).

questions.prompt: Focusing production on a narrower task or product.
questions.explanation: Specialization means concentrating productive effort on the task or good for which a person or nation has a comparative advantage. It raises output per worker (absolute productivity), enables gains from trade, and is the basis of the division of labor. On the PPC, a country that specializes along its comparative advantage and then trades can reach a consumption point outside its own frontier.

questions.prompt: Ratio comparing export prices to import prices, showing how much imports a country can buy with its exports.
questions.explanation: Terms of trade (TOT) define the rate at which one country's exports exchange for imports. Both countries gain from trade only when the TOT lie between each country's domestic opportunity cost ratios. A TOT more favorable than your domestic opportunity cost means you import at a lower cost than producing at home, boosting welfare beyond your own PPC.

questions.prompt: Model showing money, goods, services, and resources moving between households and firms.
questions.explanation: The circular flow model illustrates how households and firms interact in two markets: product markets (firms sell goods/services to households) and factor markets (households sell labor, land, and capital to firms). Money flows clockwise (spending); real flows — goods, services, and resources — flow counter-clockwise. Government, the financial sector, and foreign trade are added in more complex versions.

questions.prompt: Lower opportunity cost that explains specialization and trade gains.
questions.explanation: A producer has a comparative advantage in a good when it faces a lower opportunity cost than its trading partner — not necessarily lower absolute cost (absolute advantage). Trade is mutually beneficial when each party specializes in its comparative advantage. AP FRQ: always compute opportunity costs from the data given to determine who should specialize and the acceptable terms of trade.

questions.prompt: An economy where government makes most economic decisions.
questions.explanation: In a command economy, the government owns resources and makes the central decisions about what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom — replacing price signals with directives. Without market prices, information is lost and misallocation (shortages and surpluses) is common. Contrast with a market economy (decentralized prices) and a mixed economy (both).

questions.prompt: Quantities consumers are willing and able to buy at each price.
questions.explanation: Demand is the relationship between price and quantity demanded, holding other factors constant (ceteris paribus). The demand curve slopes downward because of the substitution effect (higher price makes other goods relatively cheaper) and the income effect (higher price reduces real purchasing power). Movement along the curve = price change; shift of the curve = change in a non-price determinant (income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, number of buyers).

questions.prompt: Quantities producers are willing and able to sell at each price.
questions.explanation: Supply is the relationship between price and quantity producers are willing and able to sell, ceteris paribus. The supply curve slopes upward: higher prices cover higher marginal costs, making additional production worthwhile. Shift the supply curve — don't move along it — when non-price determinants change: input costs, technology, number of sellers, expectations, taxes/subsidies, or prices of related-in-production goods.

questions.prompt: Legal protections over ownership and use of resources, goods, or ideas.
questions.explanation: Property rights define who can use, sell, or benefit from a resource or good. Secure, well-defined property rights are essential for markets to function: they create incentives to invest, maintain, and trade assets. When property rights are absent or unclear (e.g., common resources, public goods), externalities and market failures emerge. Patent and copyright law are property rights that encourage innovation.

questions.prompt: Buyer benefit from paying less than maximum willingness to pay.
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus (CS) is the difference between the maximum a buyer is willing to pay (their reservation price) and the actual market price paid, summed across all buyers. On a supply-demand graph, CS is the triangular area above the equilibrium price and below the demand curve. Taxes, price ceilings, and monopoly pricing reduce CS; subsidies increase it. Together with producer surplus, CS measures total market welfare.

questions.prompt: Comparing additional benefits and additional costs.
questions.explanation: Marginal analysis compares the additional (marginal) benefit of one more unit to the additional (marginal) cost. The optimal decision occurs where MB = MC; if MB > MC, expand; if MB < MC, contract. This principle drives every optimization in AP Micro — from output (MR = MC) to input hiring (MRP = MRC) to pollution control (MSB = MSC).

questions.prompt: Additional benefit gained from one more unit of an action or good.
questions.explanation: The marginal benefit (MB) curve shows the additional benefit a consumer receives from each successive unit consumed. It slopes downward due to diminishing marginal utility. In a competitive market, the demand curve IS the marginal benefit curve, since a consumer buys up to the point where MB = price. The area under the MB curve up to the quantity consumed equals total benefit.

questions.prompt: Trade-off between maximizing total output and distributing benefits more fairly.
questions.explanation: Efficiency seeks to maximize total output and minimize waste (produce on the PPC, allocate where P = MC); equity focuses on the fairness of distribution. Policies improving equity (progressive taxes, subsidies to low-income groups) often reduce efficiency by distorting incentives, and vice versa. AP Micro FRQs may ask you to evaluate a policy on both dimensions.

questions.prompt: Additional cost created by producing or choosing one more unit.
questions.explanation: The marginal cost (MC) curve shows the additional cost of producing one more unit of output. MC typically falls initially (fixed costs spreading, increasing returns) then rises as diminishing marginal returns set in — giving MC its characteristic U-shape (or upward slope in the relevant range). In perfect competition, the firm's supply curve is the MC curve above AVC; all profit-maximizing firms set MR = MC.

questions.prompt: Choosing the option where expected marginal benefits are greater than expected marginal costs.
questions.explanation: Rational decision-making assumes individuals choose the option that maximizes their net benefit — pursuing an action as long as marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost, and stopping where MB = MC. Rationality does not require perfect information; it requires responding systematically to incentives. AP Micro assumes rational actors: consumers maximize utility, firms maximize profit, governments maximize social welfare.

questions.prompt: Trade in which both sides choose to participate because each expects to gain.
questions.explanation: Voluntary exchange occurs when both parties expect to be better off — otherwise one party would refuse. It is the engine of markets and drives specialization: each party gives up something valued less in order to receive something valued more, expanding total surplus. Price is set anywhere both parties gain, bounded by each side's opportunity cost.

questions.prompt: Economy combining markets and government intervention.
questions.explanation: A mixed economy combines private market decision-making with government intervention. Markets allocate most goods via prices, but government corrects market failures (externalities, public goods, information problems), redistributes income, and enforces rules. The U.S. is a mixed economy — most allocation is market-driven, but antitrust law, environmental regulation, and social programs reflect public-sector roles.

questions.prompt: Market outcome where planned buying equals planned selling.
questions.explanation: Market equilibrium is the price-quantity combination where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied — the market 'clears.' At a price above equilibrium, surplus develops and price falls; below equilibrium, shortage develops and price rises. Equilibrium price and quantity shift whenever supply or demand changes; the new equilibrium reflects the updated market conditions.

questions.prompt: Rules and organizations, such as property rights, courts, banks, and firms, that structure economic activity.
questions.explanation: Economic institutions are the formal (laws, contracts, property rights, courts, banks) and informal (norms, customs, trust) rules that structure economic behavior. Strong institutions lower transaction costs, enforce contracts, and create incentives for investment and innovation. Weak or absent institutions — corruption, unclear property rights — lead to market failures and slower growth.

questions.prompt: Prices, profits, losses, rewards, or penalties that push buyers and sellers to change behavior.
questions.explanation: Market incentives — prices, profits, and losses — coordinate decentralized economic decisions without central planning. Rising prices signal producers to expand output and consumers to economize; profits attract entry; losses trigger exit. Government policies (taxes, subsidies, price controls) alter incentives and change behavior, sometimes causing unintended consequences (shortages, surpluses, reduced innovation).

questions.prompt: Non-price factor changing demand.
questions.explanation: A demand shifter is any non-price factor that moves the entire demand curve left or right. The main shifters (TRIBE): Tastes, Related-good prices (substitutes/complements), Income, Buyer count, and Expectations. A rightward shift means consumers want more at every price; leftward, less. Changes in price cause movement along the demand curve — not a shift.

questions.prompt: Goods for which demand rises when consumer income rises.
questions.explanation: A normal good is one for which demand increases as consumer income rises (positive income elasticity of demand, YED > 0). Most goods are normal — as incomes rise, people buy more restaurant meals, vacations, and new cars. Contrast with inferior goods (YED < 0), for which demand falls when income rises. Luxury goods are a subset of normal goods with YED > 1.

questions.prompt: Movement of the entire demand curve caused by a non-price determinant of demand.
questions.explanation: A shift in the demand curve means the entire schedule changes: at every price, consumers now demand a different quantity. Rightward shifts (demand increase) follow from: higher income (for normal goods), higher prices of substitutes, lower prices of complements, favorable taste changes, higher expected future prices, or more buyers. Leftward shifts reflect the opposites. Do not confuse a demand shift with a price-driven movement along the curve.

questions.prompt: Government payment that lowers costs and encourages production or consumption.
questions.explanation: A subsidy is a government payment to producers or consumers that lowers the effective price paid or cost incurred. A producer subsidy shifts supply rightward by the subsidy amount, reducing equilibrium price and increasing equilibrium quantity. A consumer subsidy shifts demand rightward. Subsidies increase total quantity, raise producer and consumer surplus, but create DWL (overproduction relative to the efficient quantity) and cost taxpayers the subsidy × Q.

questions.prompt: Goods for which demand falls when consumer income rises.
questions.explanation: An inferior good is one for which demand falls as consumer income rises (negative income elasticity, YED < 0). Examples: instant noodles, bus rides, generic-brand products. As income rises, consumers substitute toward higher-quality normal goods. AP FRQ trap: 'inferior' is an economic term (demand falls with income), NOT a statement about product quality. Do not confuse with Giffen goods (demand rises as price rises — a special inferior good).

questions.prompt: Goods that can replace each other, so a higher price for one raises demand for the other.
questions.explanation: Substitute goods satisfy similar wants, so they compete for consumer dollars. If the price of Good A rises, demand for substitute Good B increases (positive cross-price elasticity). Examples: Coke and Pepsi, butter and margarine, Uber and Lyft. AP tip: for producers, goods that are substitutes in production compete for the same inputs — producing more of one reduces supply of the other (e.g., wheat and corn on the same farmland).

questions.prompt: Measure of how demand for one good responds to another good's price.
questions.explanation: Cross-price elasticity of demand (XED) measures how the quantity demanded of one good responds to a price change in another: XED = %ΔQd of Good A / %ΔP of Good B. Positive XED → substitutes (higher price of B raises demand for A). Negative XED → complements (higher price of B reduces demand for A). Zero XED → unrelated goods. The magnitude indicates strength of relationship.

questions.prompt: Legal maximum price.
questions.explanation: A price ceiling is a government-imposed maximum legal price below equilibrium. Because the ceiling keeps price artificially low, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, creating a shortage. Effects: shortages, non-price rationing (queues, discrimination), reduced quality, black markets. An effective (binding) ceiling must be set below equilibrium; a ceiling above equilibrium is non-binding and has no effect. AP example: rent control.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing how a tax burden is divided between buyers and sellers.
questions.explanation: The tax incidence graph shows supply shifting left by the per-unit tax amount. Buyers pay Pb (higher than P*) and sellers receive Ps (lower than P*); the tax wedge = Pb − Ps = the tax rate. Tax revenue = tax × Q*new (the rectangle); DWL = the triangle. The relative burden on buyers vs. sellers depends on elasticities: the more inelastic side bears more. With perfectly inelastic demand, buyers bear 100% of the tax.

questions.prompt: Legal minimum set above equilibrium that can create surplus.
questions.explanation: A price floor is a government-imposed minimum legal price above equilibrium. Because the floor keeps price artificially high, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, creating a surplus. Effects: unsold surplus, reduced transactions, potential side payments or quality upgrades. An effective (binding) floor must be set above equilibrium. AP examples: minimum wage (labor market floor), agricultural price supports.

questions.prompt: Legal maximum price that creates a shortage when set below equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A binding (effective) price ceiling is set below equilibrium price, so it actually constrains the market. At the ceiling price, Qd > Qs, generating a persistent shortage. Without the ceiling, the price would rise to clear the market; with it, non-price rationing mechanisms (first-come-first-served, favoritism) allocate the scarce supply. DWL results from transactions that no longer occur above the ceiling price.

questions.prompt: Legal minimum price that creates a surplus when set above equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A binding (effective) price floor is set above equilibrium price, directly raising the market price above P*. At the floor, Qs > Qd, creating a surplus the market cannot clear on its own. Government often must purchase the surplus (agricultural price supports) or mandate third-party purchasing. DWL = lost surplus from units not traded between the floor quantity supplied and the lower equilibrium quantity.

questions.prompt: Goods used together, so a higher price for one lowers demand for the other.
questions.explanation: Complement goods are used together, so a price increase in one reduces demand for both. Cross-price elasticity of complements is negative: if the price of printers rises, demand for printer ink falls. Examples: cars and gasoline, peanut butter and jelly, smartphones and apps. AP FRQ: complements in production are goods produced jointly — higher output of beef → more leather (positive supply relationship).

questions.prompt: Per-unit tax that raises costs, reduces quantity, and splits burden between buyers and sellers.
questions.explanation: An excise (per-unit) tax shifts the supply curve upward by the tax amount, creating a wedge between the price buyers pay (Pb) and the price sellers receive (Ps = Pb − tax). The tax burden is shared: buyers pay more, sellers receive less. The split depends on relative elasticities — the more inelastic side bears more of the burden. Tax revenue = tax × Q*new; DWL = the triangle between the old and new quantities.

questions.prompt: Demand that responds weakly to price changes.
questions.explanation: Demand is inelastic when the price elasticity of demand (PED) has an absolute value less than 1: a 1% price increase causes less than 1% drop in quantity demanded. Necessities with few substitutes (insulin, gasoline, cigarettes) tend to be inelastic. Sellers bear less tax burden when demand is inelastic; higher price raises total revenue (TR) for sellers. AP FRQ: to determine elasticity, compare %ΔQ with %ΔP.

questions.prompt: Inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded.
questions.explanation: The law of demand states that, ceteris paribus, quantity demanded falls as price rises (and rises as price falls) — producing a downward-sloping demand curve. Two effects explain it: the substitution effect (a higher price makes the good relatively expensive, pushing buyers to alternatives) and the income effect (a higher price reduces real purchasing power). Giffen goods are a rare exception the AP exam does not require.

questions.prompt: Movement of the entire supply curve caused by a non-price determinant of supply.
questions.explanation: A shift in supply means the entire supply schedule changes — sellers offer a different quantity at every price. Rightward shifts (supply increase): lower input costs, better technology, more sellers, subsidies, or favorable price expectations. Leftward shifts (supply decrease): higher input costs, taxes, fewer sellers. AP FRQ trap: a price change moves you along the supply curve; only a non-price determinant shifts the curve.

questions.prompt: Non-price factor changing supply.
questions.explanation: A supply shifter is any non-price factor that moves the entire supply curve. Key shifters: input prices (higher wages → left shift), technology (better tech → right shift), number of sellers, expectations of future prices, taxes (left) and subsidies (right), related-in-production goods. A rightward shift means sellers offer more at every price; a leftward shift means less.

questions.prompt: Direct relationship between price and quantity supplied.
questions.explanation: The law of supply states that, ceteris paribus, quantity supplied rises as price rises — producing an upward-sloping supply curve. Higher prices cover higher marginal costs (rising MC due to diminishing marginal returns), making additional production profitable. AP FRQ: a 'change in supply' = shift in the curve; a 'change in quantity supplied' = movement along the curve in response to a price change.

questions.prompt: Decision-making by buyers as they compare prices, benefits, income limits, and opportunity costs.
questions.explanation: Consumers maximize utility (satisfaction) subject to a budget constraint by choosing quantities where marginal utility per dollar is equalized across all goods: MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B. Demand curves are derived from this optimization: as price rises, the good's MU/P falls, so the consumer buys less. AP Micro applications: deriving demand from utility, income and substitution effects, and the rationale for the downward-sloping demand curve.

questions.prompt: Responsiveness of quantity supplied to price changes.
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of supply (PES) = %ΔQs / %ΔP. PES > 1 = elastic supply (firms can quickly ramp up output); PES < 1 = inelastic supply (supply is slow to respond). Time is the key determinant: in the very short run, supply is perfectly inelastic (fixed capacity); in the long run, supply is more elastic as firms can adjust all inputs. AP FRQ: elastic supply means taxes fall more on buyers (demand side).

questions.prompt: Demand that responds strongly to price changes.
questions.explanation: Demand is elastic when the absolute value of PED > 1: a 1% price increase causes more than 1% decrease in quantity demanded. Goods with many close substitutes, large budget shares, or non-urgent needs tend to be elastic. For elastic demand, a price increase reduces total revenue (TR = P × Q falls because Q drops sharply). AP FRQ tip: to determine elasticity from a graph, compare %ΔQ with %ΔP, or use the midpoint formula.

questions.prompt: Measure of how demand responds to income changes.
questions.explanation: Income elasticity of demand (YED) = %ΔQd / %ΔIncome. Positive YED → normal good; negative YED → inferior good; YED > 1 → luxury (demand rises faster than income). Examples: YED for fine dining ≈ +2 (luxury); YED for bus rides ≈ −0.5 (inferior). AP FRQ: if income rises 10% and demand rises 5%, YED = 0.5 (normal, income-inelastic). Used in business forecasting and policy analysis.

questions.prompt: Responsiveness of quantity demanded to price changes.
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand (PED) = %ΔQd / %ΔP (absolute value), calculated using the midpoint formula to avoid direction bias. |PED| > 1 = elastic; |PED| < 1 = inelastic; |PED| = 1 = unit elastic. Determinants: availability of substitutes (more → more elastic), time horizon (longer → more elastic), share of budget, and necessity vs. luxury. Revenue rule: price ↑ raises TR if inelastic, lowers TR if elastic.

questions.prompt: Public action, such as taxes, subsidies, regulation, or price controls, that changes market outcomes.
questions.explanation: Government intervention modifies market outcomes through price controls (ceilings/floors), taxes, subsidies, regulations, or public provision. Each tool distorts the equilibrium price and quantity, creating winners and losers and usually generating DWL. Intervention is justified when markets fail (externalities, public goods, information problems, monopoly power); the AP FRQ often asks you to graph the intervention and identify surplus changes and DWL.

questions.prompt: Relationship between inputs and output.
questions.explanation: The production function Q = f(L, K) shows the maximum output achievable from given quantities of labor (L) and capital (K). In the short run, at least one input is fixed; in the long run, all inputs are variable. The production function drives cost curves: as diminishing marginal returns to labor set in (MPL falls), MC rises, explaining the U-shape of MC, AVC, and ATC curves.

questions.prompt: Additional output from one more input.
questions.explanation: Marginal product (MP) — most often marginal product of labor (MPL) — is the additional output gained by adding one more unit of a variable input, holding other inputs constant: MPL = ΔQ/ΔL. Initially MP rises (increasing returns) as workers specialize, then falls due to diminishing marginal returns as more workers share fixed capital. When MP = 0, total product is maximized; negative MP means total output is falling.

questions.prompt: Outcome where goods are produced at the lowest possible cost.
questions.explanation: Productive efficiency means producing a given output at the lowest possible average total cost — operating at the minimum point of the ATC curve where ATC = MC. In long-run perfect competition, market forces drive all firms to this minimum-ATC output (P = min ATC). Monopolistic competitors are productively inefficient in the long run: they produce on the downward-sloping part of ATC (excess capacity).

questions.prompt: Outcome where resources go to the uses consumers value most, often where price equals marginal cost.
questions.explanation: Allocative efficiency means resources flow to their highest-valued uses, achieved when P = MC — the price consumers pay exactly equals the marginal cost of production. At this output, no resource can be reallocated to make anyone better off without making someone worse off (Pareto optimality). Perfect competition achieves allocative efficiency (P = MR = MC); monopoly does not (P > MC), generating deadweight loss.

questions.prompt: Additional cost created by producing or choosing one more unit.
questions.explanation: The MC curve shows the additional cost of each successive unit of output: MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = ΔTVC/ΔQ. MC falls initially due to increasing marginal returns then rises as diminishing returns set in — giving MC its U-shape. The MC curve passes through the minimum of AVC and ATC. For a price-taking firm, the MC curve above AVC is the firm's short-run supply curve.

questions.prompt: Variable cost per unit of output, found by dividing variable cost by quantity.
questions.explanation: Average variable cost (AVC) = TVC / Q. Like ATC, AVC is U-shaped: initially falls due to increasing returns, hits a minimum where MC = AVC, then rises. AVC is critical for the shutdown decision: if P < min AVC, the firm covers none of its variable costs and minimizes losses by shutting down. The MC curve cuts AVC from below at AVC's minimum, which is the shutdown point of the competitive firm's supply curve.

questions.prompt: Total cost per unit.
questions.explanation: Average total cost (ATC) = TC / Q = AFC + AVC. The ATC curve is U-shaped: initially falls as fixed costs spread over more units (AFC effect dominates), reaches a minimum where MC = ATC, then rises as rising variable costs dominate. In long-run perfect competition, P = min ATC (zero economic profit). Profit per unit = P − ATC; total profit = (P − ATC) × Q — the rectangle on the graph.

questions.prompt: Fixed cost per unit of output, found by dividing fixed cost by quantity.
questions.explanation: Average fixed cost (AFC) = TFC / Q, which falls continuously as output rises — a rectangular hyperbola. AFC never reaches zero but approaches it asymptotically. Because AFC = ATC − AVC, the vertical gap between the ATC and AVC curves equals AFC; this gap narrows as output increases. AFC has no minimum point and doesn't factor into the shutdown or exit decision — fixed costs are sunk in the short run.

questions.prompt: Cost that changes with output.
questions.explanation: Variable costs (VC) change with the level of output — raw materials, hourly labor wages, utilities proportional to production. TVC = 0 at zero output and rises with Q. AVC = TVC/Q; MC = ΔTVC/ΔQ. Because fixed costs are sunk, only variable costs are relevant to the short-run output decision. The area under the MC curve up to any quantity equals total variable cost at that output level.

questions.prompt: Short-run decision to stop production when price is below average variable cost.
questions.explanation: A firm should shut down in the short run when P < min AVC: continuing to produce would lose more than just the fixed costs. As long as P ≥ AVC, the firm covers all variable costs and contributes something toward fixed costs — operating is better than shutting down. The shutdown point is the minimum of AVC on the graph. In the long run, the firm exits if P < ATC (economic losses persist even after full adjustment).

questions.prompt: Firm or buyer that must accept the market price because it has no power to set price.
questions.explanation: A price taker is a firm (or buyer) that must accept the market-determined price because its individual output is so small relative to total market output that it cannot influence price. Price takers face a perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve at the market price, so P = MR for every unit sold. All firms in perfect competition are price takers. Contrast with a price maker (monopoly, oligopoly) which faces a downward-sloping demand curve.

questions.prompt: Market with many firms selling identical products.
questions.explanation: Perfect competition features: many buyers and sellers, identical (homogeneous) products, perfect information, no barriers to entry or exit, and firms are price takers (P = MR). The firm's demand curve is horizontal (perfectly elastic) at the market price. Short-run equilibrium: MR = MC; long-run equilibrium: P = MR = MC = min ATC. At long-run equilibrium, economic profit = 0, productive and allocative efficiency both hold.

questions.prompt: Condition where firms can enter or leave a market without major barriers.
questions.explanation: Free entry and exit means no legal, financial, or technological barriers prevent firms from entering or leaving the market. This condition drives perfect competition toward long-run zero economic profit: positive profits attract entrants (supply increases, P falls); losses cause exits (supply decreases, P rises). Without free entry/exit, incumbent firms can sustain economic profit long-term — the basis of monopoly and oligopoly power.

questions.prompt: Minimum profit needed to keep resources in their current use.
questions.explanation: Normal profit is the minimum return needed to keep the entrepreneur's resources in their current use — it equals the opportunity cost of owner-supplied inputs. When a firm earns normal profit, economic profit = 0 (TR = TC including all implicit costs). In long-run perfect competition, all firms earn exactly normal profit. Above normal profit (positive economic profit) attracts entry; below it (negative economic profit) triggers exit.

questions.prompt: Market state after full adjustment, with firms making normal profit in perfect competition.
questions.explanation: In long-run perfect competition equilibrium, P = MR = MC = min ATC. Economic profit is zero (normal profit only) because positive profits attract new entrants (shifting market supply right, lowering price) and losses cause exit (shifting supply left, raising price) until P = min ATC. This outcome achieves both productive efficiency (min ATC) and allocative efficiency (P = MC). AP FRQ: draw the adjustment process from short-run profit/loss to long-run equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Additional cost of producing one more unit.
questions.explanation: Marginal cost (MC) = ΔTC/ΔQ — the change in total cost from producing one additional unit of output. Since fixed costs don't change, MC also equals ΔTVC/ΔQ. MC intersects AVC and ATC at their respective minimums. Every profit-maximizing firm (competitive or not) produces where MR = MC; below this output MB > MC (expand), above it MC > MR (cut back). MC is the pivotal cost concept in AP Micro.

questions.prompt: Profit remaining after subtracting explicit and implicit costs.
questions.explanation: Economic profit = Total Revenue − Total Economic Cost (explicit + implicit costs). Implicit costs include the opportunity cost of owner-supplied resources (foregone wages, rent, return on capital). A firm can earn positive accounting profit but zero or negative economic profit. Zero economic profit = normal profit (the firm earns exactly enough to keep resources in this use). Positive economic profit attracts entry; negative profit causes exit.

questions.prompt: Output choice where marginal revenue equals marginal cost.
questions.explanation: A profit-maximizing firm produces the quantity where MR = MC (and MC is rising). Below MR = MC output: each extra unit adds more revenue than cost — produce more. Above MR = MC: each extra unit costs more than it earns — cut back. For perfect competitors, MR = P so the rule is P = MC. For monopolists, MR < P, so the profit-max Q is lower than the competitive Q, resulting in P > MC and deadweight loss.

questions.prompt: Graph showing the additional revenue earned from selling one more unit.
questions.explanation: The marginal revenue (MR) curve shows additional revenue from selling one more unit: MR = ΔTR/ΔQ. For a perfect competitor (price taker), MR = P (horizontal MR = demand curve). For a monopolist or monopolistic competitor, the demand curve slopes down, so selling one more unit requires lowering the price on all units — MR < P and the MR curve lies below demand, twice as steep when demand is linear. Firms set MR = MC to maximize profit.

questions.prompt: Comparison of total revenue and total cost to determine whether a firm earns profit or loss.
questions.explanation: Economic profit (or loss) = TR − TC = (P − ATC) × Q. On a cost-curve graph: if P > ATC at the profit-max quantity, the firm earns profit (rectangle above ATC); if P < ATC, it incurs a loss (rectangle below P). If P < AVC, shut down in the short run; if P < ATC but ≥ AVC, operate (cover variable costs plus some fixed costs). AP FRQ: shade the profit/loss rectangle correctly using P, ATC, and Q.

questions.prompt: Total quantity of output produced by a firm with a given amount of inputs.
questions.explanation: Total product (TP) is the total quantity of output produced from a given set of inputs, typically plotted against the variable input (labor): TP = Q(L). TP first rises at an increasing rate (increasing marginal returns), then rises at a decreasing rate (diminishing marginal returns), reaches a maximum (MP = 0), and may fall (MP < 0). The slope of the TP curve at any point equals MPL.

questions.prompt: Declining extra output from additional variable inputs.
questions.explanation: The law of diminishing marginal returns (short-run) states that as more units of a variable input (labor) are added to fixed inputs (capital), the marginal product eventually decreases. This occurs because additional workers share the same fixed capital. Diminishing returns drive MC upward, explaining the rising portion of the U-shaped MC and AVC curves. Long-run economies/diseconomies of scale are a distinct concept — all inputs vary.

questions.prompt: Per-unit cost curve when all inputs can change and firms can adjust scale.
questions.explanation: The long-run average total cost (LRATC) curve is the envelope of all possible short-run ATC curves, showing minimum per-unit cost when all inputs are variable. It exhibits economies of scale (LRATC falls) where larger scale reduces average cost, constant returns to scale (flat LRATC), and diseconomies of scale (LRATC rises) at very large output. Natural monopolies have LRATC falling over the entire relevant output range.

questions.prompt: Cost that stays unchanged as output rises or falls.
questions.explanation: Fixed costs (FC) do not change as output changes in the short run — rent, loan payments, equipment leases. AFC = FC/Q, which falls continuously as output rises. Because FC is the same at all output levels, it does not affect MC (MC = ΔTVC/ΔQ) and thus does not affect the profit-maximizing output choice. In the short run, fixed costs are sunk and irrelevant to output decisions; in the long run, all costs are variable.

questions.prompt: Applied choice where gaining one benefit requires accepting a cost or giving up an alternative.
questions.explanation: Real-world trade-offs apply the PPC framework to actual decisions: a firm choosing between two products, a student choosing study time vs. leisure, a government choosing defense vs. social spending. Each choice involves an opportunity cost — the value of the next-best alternative foregone. AP FRQ: trade-offs appear in efficiency vs. equity questions, short-run vs. long-run cost decisions, and comparisons between market structures.

questions.prompt: Market with one seller and barriers to entry.
questions.explanation: A monopoly is a market with one seller of a good with no close substitutes, protected by high barriers to entry. The monopolist faces the entire downward-sloping market demand curve, making it a price maker — it chooses output where MR = MC and charges the price consumers are willing to pay (from the demand curve). Result: Q < competitive Q, P > MC, positive economic profit, deadweight loss, and allocative inefficiency.

questions.prompt: Market where one firm can supply output at lower cost than many firms.
questions.explanation: A natural monopoly exists when LRATC falls over the entire relevant range of output — one large firm can supply the whole market at lower per-unit cost than multiple competing firms (economies of scale dominate). Examples: electricity transmission, water distribution, railways. Government typically regulates natural monopolies: marginal-cost pricing (P = MC, achieving allocative efficiency) often requires a subsidy since MC < ATC; average-cost pricing (P = ATC) breaks even but causes slight DWL.

questions.prompt: Government action addressing a public issue.
questions.explanation: Public policy in the context of imperfect competition includes antitrust enforcement, price regulation of natural monopolies, consumer protection laws, and merger review. AP Micro FRQs may ask you to evaluate a policy's effect on price, output, profit, consumer surplus, and DWL. Key judgment: does the policy move the market closer to P = MC (allocative efficiency) and minimum ATC (productive efficiency)?

questions.prompt: Market dominated by a few interdependent firms.
questions.explanation: An oligopoly is a market dominated by a few large, interdependent firms (e.g., automotive, airline, telecom industries). Interdependence means each firm's pricing and output decisions depend on anticipated rival responses. Oligopolies can behave competitively (price wars, Bertrand) or collusively (cartel, Cournot). Market outcomes range from near-competitive to near-monopoly depending on collusion, barriers to entry, and product differentiation.

questions.prompt: Game outcome where no player can improve by changing strategy alone.
questions.explanation: A Nash equilibrium is a set of strategies where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing strategy, given what others are doing. It is the stable solution concept of game theory. Multiple Nash equilibria may exist; some games have only mixed-strategy Nash equilibria. In oligopoly, Nash equilibrium often yields outcomes between the collusive (monopoly) and competitive extremes — e.g., Cournot equilibrium. Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium, but not vice versa.

questions.prompt: Obstacles that make it difficult for new firms to enter a market.
questions.explanation: Barriers to entry prevent new competitors from entering a market, allowing incumbent firms to sustain long-run economic profit. Types: natural (economies of scale creating a natural monopoly), legal (patents, licenses, copyrights), strategic (predatory pricing, exclusive contracts, brand loyalty), and resource control (one firm owns a key input). High barriers → monopoly or oligopoly; no barriers → long-run zero economic profit (perfect competition).

questions.prompt: Decision-making that depends on how rivals are expected to respond.
questions.explanation: Strategic behavior in oligopoly means each firm's decisions account for expected reactions of rivals. Unlike perfect competition (firms ignore rivals) or monopoly (no rivals), oligopolists must anticipate retaliation: if Firm A lowers price, will Firm B follow? Game theory formalizes this: payoff matrices, dominant strategies, and Nash equilibria model how rational firms interact. AP FRQ: set up a payoff matrix and identify the Nash equilibrium and dominant strategies.

questions.prompt: Charging different prices to different consumers for the same product.
questions.explanation: Price discrimination means charging different consumers different prices for the same good based on their willingness to pay, not cost differences. It requires market power (downward-sloping demand), ability to identify consumer segments, and prevention of resale. 1st-degree (perfect): charge each consumer their reservation price — extracts all CS, eliminates DWL. 3rd-degree: segment markets (students, seniors, airlines). AP FRQ: 1st-degree discrimination raises output to competitive Q and converts CS to producer surplus.

questions.prompt: Lost total surplus from inefficient output.
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss (DWL) in imperfect competition is the surplus lost because output is restricted below the allocatively efficient quantity (where D = MC). For a monopoly, DWL = the triangle between Q_m and Q_competitive, bounded by the demand and MC curves. Oligopolies also generate DWL when they act collectively to restrict output. Greater market power (lower output restriction) → larger DWL. Policy aim: reduce DWL through antitrust, regulation, or entry promotion.

questions.prompt: Public action, such as taxes, subsidies, regulation, or price controls, that changes market outcomes.
questions.explanation: Government intervention in imperfect competition aims to reduce market power abuses: antitrust enforcement (breakups, fines, merger blocks), rate-of-return regulation for natural monopolies (cap P at ATC), and marginal-cost pricing requirements (P = MC, efficient but may require subsidy). AP FRQ: evaluate each policy's effect on P, Q, CS, PS, DWL, and firm profit — recognize that no intervention is costless.

questions.prompt: Ability of one firm to influence price because of barriers to entry and limited competition.
questions.explanation: Monopoly power is the ability to set price above marginal cost — the hallmark of any firm facing a downward-sloping demand curve (monopoly, oligopoly, or monopolistic competitor). It arises from barriers to entry, brand loyalty, geographic isolation, or product differentiation. Measured by the Lerner Index: (P − MC)/P. Greater monopoly power → larger markup, larger DWL, and larger profit. Perfect competitors have zero monopoly power (P = MC).

questions.prompt: Strategy of making a good seem distinct through quality, design, location, service, or branding.
questions.explanation: Product differentiation means making a product seem unique through quality, design, location, service, branding, or advertising, giving the firm some market power (a downward-sloping demand curve rather than horizontal). It is the defining feature of monopolistic competition and a key oligopoly strategy. Differentiation allows firms to charge P > MC without losing all customers, though in the long run entry drives profit to zero in monopolistic competition.

questions.prompt: Market with many firms selling differentiated products.
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition features: many sellers, differentiated products (giving each firm some market power), low barriers to entry and exit. Short run: firms can earn economic profit or loss (like monopoly). Long run: entry/exit drives economic profit to zero (like perfect competition), but P > MC and P > min ATC persist — the market is neither allocatively nor productively efficient. AP example: restaurants, hair salons, retail clothing.

questions.prompt: Graph showing the additional revenue earned from selling one more unit.
questions.explanation: For any firm facing a downward-sloping demand curve (monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition), MR lies below demand: selling one more unit requires lowering the price on all previous units, so MR < P. For a linear demand curve, the MR curve has the same intercept but twice the slope. The gap between P and MR grows as output increases. AP FRQ: always draw MR starting at the same price-axis intercept as demand but reaching zero at half the quantity where demand hits the x-axis.

questions.prompt: Competition through advertising, quality, service, or branding instead of lower prices.
questions.explanation: Nonprice competition means firms compete through product quality, advertising, customer service, location, brand image, and innovation rather than cutting price. It is prevalent in monopolistic competition and oligopoly, where firms seek to shift their individual demand curves rightward (gain market share) without triggering price wars. While it can provide consumers information and variety, advertising raises costs and can create artificial differentiation.

questions.prompt: Study of strategic decision-making.
questions.explanation: Game theory models strategic interactions where each player's outcome depends on the choices of others. Core concepts: players, strategies, payoffs, and equilibria. The payoff matrix shows outcomes for all strategy combinations. Used to analyze oligopoly behavior, arms races, trade policy, and auctions. Key results: dominant strategy (best regardless of rival), Nash equilibrium (stable, no unilateral incentive to deviate), and Prisoner's Dilemma (individually rational strategies lead to collectively worse outcomes).

questions.prompt: Firm graph with a downward-sloping demand curve, MR below demand, and zero long-run economic profit.
questions.explanation: Short run: firm has downward-sloping D and MR below D; sets MR = MC; earns economic profit or loss. Long run: entry (if profit) or exit (if loss) shifts each firm's demand left (entry) or right (exit) until demand is tangent to ATC — zero economic profit. At tangency, P = ATC > min ATC: firm is on the downward-sloping ATC section → excess capacity (not productively efficient) and P > MC → allocative inefficiency and DWL.

questions.prompt: Lost total surplus caused by producing less or more than the efficient quantity.
questions.explanation: The DWL triangle is the graphical representation of lost total surplus due to market inefficiency. For a monopoly: the triangle between Q_monopoly and Q_competitive, bounded above by the demand curve and below by the MC curve. For a tax: the triangle between the post-tax and pre-tax quantities, bounded by supply and demand. The DWL triangle represents mutually beneficial transactions that do not occur because the market is restricted from reaching the efficient output.

questions.prompt: Game in which individual incentives push players toward a worse collective outcome.
questions.explanation: The Prisoner's Dilemma is a game where each player has a dominant strategy (e.g., defect/cheat) that leads to a Nash equilibrium worse for both than if they had cooperated. Applied to oligopoly: two firms each have an incentive to undercut or cheat on a cartel agreement, even though mutual cooperation yields higher joint profits. The dilemma explains why cartels are unstable — each member gains by cheating, so the cooperative outcome is not self-enforcing without binding agreements.

questions.prompt: Use of laws and agencies to limit monopoly power, collusion, and anti-competitive mergers.
questions.explanation: Antitrust enforcement applies antitrust laws to prevent monopolization, collusion, and mergers that substantially lessen competition. The FTC and DOJ Antitrust Division are the key U.S. agencies. Tools: merger review (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index as a concentration measure), consent decrees, fines, and structural remedies (breaking up firms). Modern enforcement also targets digital platform monopolies and exclusionary conduct under Section 2 of the Sherman Act.

questions.prompt: Firms cooperating to raise prices or restrict output.
questions.explanation: Collusion occurs when firms coordinate pricing, output, or market allocation to mimic monopoly outcomes, maximizing joint profit at the expense of consumers and allocative efficiency. Explicit collusion (price-fixing cartels) is illegal under U.S. antitrust law. Tacit collusion (price leadership, signaling) is harder to prosecute. Cartels are inherently unstable: each member has a short-run incentive to cheat — produce more than agreed and free-ride on the cartel price.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing a monopolist choosing output where MR equals MC and charging price from the demand curve.
questions.explanation: The monopoly graph shows: downward-sloping demand (D) and MR curves, with MR below D (twice as steep for linear demand); MC curve (U-shaped); profit-max at Q_m where MR = MC; price P_m from demand above Q_m. Profit = (P_m − ATC) × Q_m (shaded rectangle). DWL = triangle between Q_m and Q_competitive (where D = MC). AP FRQ: label all curves, Q_m, P_m, profit rectangle, and DWL triangle.

questions.prompt: Group of firms coordinating output and price.
questions.explanation: A cartel is a formal agreement among firms to coordinate output and prices, behaving collectively like a monopolist to maximize joint profit. OPEC is the classic example. Cartels restrict output below competitive Q, raising P above MC and generating economic profit — but also DWL. Instability: each member can earn more by secretly increasing output while others hold back, making cartels prone to cheating and collapse. Explicit cartels are illegal in the U.S.

questions.prompt: Government policy limiting monopolies and anti-competitive behavior.
questions.explanation: Antitrust policy uses laws and enforcement (Sherman Act 1890, Clayton Act 1914, FTC Act) to prevent monopolization, price-fixing cartels, and anti-competitive mergers. The DOJ and FTC review large mergers; collusion is per se illegal. Remedies include fines, breakups (AT&T 1984), and injunctions. AP Micro context: antitrust enforcement aims to move market outcomes closer to competitive equilibrium, reducing prices and DWL.

questions.prompt: Curve showing how much one firm can sell at each price.
questions.explanation: The demand curve facing a single imperfectly competitive firm is downward-sloping because the firm is the only (or main) seller of a differentiated product. To sell more, it must lower price — unlike the perfectly competitive firm's horizontal demand curve. This downward slope creates the gap between P and MR, drives the output restriction relative to PC, and generates the welfare losses (DWL, CS transfer to profit) associated with market power.

questions.prompt: Legal maximum price that creates a shortage when set below equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A binding price ceiling is set below equilibrium price, forcing the market price below P*. At that price, Qd > Qs, creating a persistent shortage. Non-price rationing (queues, waiting lists) substitutes for the price mechanism. In labor factor markets, a wage ceiling below equilibrium wages would cause a labor shortage; in rent-controlled housing markets, a below-equilibrium ceiling creates a housing shortage.

questions.prompt: Price of labor.
questions.explanation: The wage rate is the price of labor — the compensation workers receive per unit of time or per unit of output. In a competitive labor market, the equilibrium wage is set where labor supply = labor demand (MRP = MRC = w). Factors raising wages: higher product prices (MRP increases), higher MPL (technology), reduced labor supply (emigration), or union bargaining. AP FRQ: show wage and employment effects of a minimum wage, monopsony, or union using labor market graphs.

questions.prompt: Legal minimum price that creates a surplus when set above equilibrium.
questions.explanation: A binding price floor is set above equilibrium price. In factor markets, the minimum wage is a price floor in the labor market — set above equilibrium wage, it creates a labor surplus (unemployment): Qs of labor > Qd of labor. Workers willing to work at the minimum wage exceed the number of jobs offered. The employment effect depends on elasticity of labor demand.

questions.prompt: Goods used together, so a higher price for one lowers demand for the other.
questions.explanation: Complement goods are consumed together (negative XED). In production, complementary inputs are used jointly — e.g., trucks and truck drivers, software and software engineers. If the price (rental rate) of a complementary capital input falls, firms demand more capital AND more of the complementary labor, shifting labor demand right. Understanding complement vs. substitute inputs is essential for factor market shift analysis.

questions.prompt: Measure of how demand for one good responds to another good's price.
questions.explanation: Cross-price elasticity (XED) = %ΔQd of Good A / %ΔP of Good B. In factor markets, positive XED between capital and labor means they are substitutes in production (higher wages → more capital demand). Negative XED means they are complements in production (e.g., specialized software needs skilled programmers — higher software prices → more demand for programmers). XED signs identify production relationships critical for factor market analysis.

questions.prompt: Market for resources such as labor, land, and capital.
questions.explanation: Factor markets are where firms buy the inputs of production — labor, land, and capital — from households (in the circular flow). Factor prices are wages (labor), rent (land), interest (capital), and profit (entrepreneurship). Factor demand is derived demand — firms hire factors based on the revenue the factor generates, not direct consumer satisfaction. The intersection of factor supply and factor demand sets the factor price (wage, rent, etc.).

questions.prompt: Per-unit tax that raises costs, reduces quantity, and splits burden between buyers and sellers.
questions.explanation: An excise (per-unit) tax on a product reduces the product's price received by firms, which lowers the MRP of labor producing that product: MRP = MPL × (P − tax effect). This shifts labor demand left in the factor market, reducing both employment and wages. AP FRQ application: a tax on output → lower product price → lower MRP → lower labor demand → lower wages and employment, shown by a leftward shift of the labor demand (MRP) curve.

questions.prompt: Goods that can replace each other, so a higher price for one raises demand for the other.
questions.explanation: Substitute goods satisfy similar wants; a price increase in one raises demand for the other (positive cross-price elasticity). In factor markets, capital can be a substitute for labor: if wages rise, firms may substitute toward capital (automation), reducing labor demand. Understanding factor substitutability is key to analyzing wage effects of minimum wage laws, automation, and immigration.

questions.prompt: Movement of the entire supply curve caused by a non-price determinant of supply.
questions.explanation: In labor markets, the labor supply curve shifts when: the working-age population changes (immigration shifts S right), preferences for work vs. leisure change, wages in alternative jobs change (higher wages elsewhere shift labor supply left for this market), or education and training completion rates change. Rightward labor supply shifts lower the equilibrium wage; leftward shifts raise it.

questions.prompt: Market where workers supply labor and firms demand labor.
questions.explanation: The labor market is where workers (households) supply labor and firms demand labor, with the equilibrium wage and employment determined by intersection of labor supply and labor demand curves. Competitive labor market: each firm is a wage taker (MRC = w), hires where MRP = w. Shifts in labor demand come from product demand and MPL changes; shifts in labor supply from population, preferences, and alternative wages.

questions.prompt: Extra revenue from using one more unit of a resource.
questions.explanation: Marginal Revenue Product (MRP) = MPL × MR — the additional revenue generated by hiring one more unit of a factor. For a competitive output market, MR = P, so MRP = MPL × P (= VMP, value of marginal product). MRP is the firm's factor demand curve: hire units as long as MRP ≥ MRC (wage). MRP shifts when: product price changes, technology changes (MPL changes), or capital changes. AP FRQ: derive MRP from a table and find the profit-maximizing employment level.

questions.prompt: Movement of the entire demand curve caused by a non-price determinant of demand.
questions.explanation: A shift in the demand curve (for a factor or a final good) occurs when a non-price determinant changes, moving the entire schedule. For factor markets, the demand for labor shifts when: the price of the final good changes, the marginal product of labor changes (technology), or the number of hiring firms changes. Rightward shift = greater demand at every wage; leftward = less demand at every wage.

questions.prompt: Government payment that lowers costs and encourages production or consumption.
questions.explanation: A subsidy to producers of a good increases their effective product price, raising MRP = MPL × P_effective. This shifts labor demand right in the factor market, raising both employment and wages. An input subsidy (e.g., job training credit) lowers the effective wage cost, shifting labor demand right or labor supply right. AP context: subsidies for education increase human capital, shifting labor supply rightward and raising productivity.

questions.prompt: Input mix that produces output at minimum cost.
questions.explanation: The least-cost input combination occurs where MPL/w = MPK/r — the marginal product per dollar is equalized across inputs. If MPL/w > MPK/r, the firm gets more output per dollar from labor, so it should substitute labor for capital until equality is restored. This is analogous to consumer utility maximization (MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B). AP FRQ: given MPL, MPK, w, and r, determine if the firm should hire more labor or capital.

questions.prompt: Hiring resources until marginal revenue product equals marginal resource cost.
questions.explanation: The profit-maximizing input hiring rule: employ a factor up to the quantity where MRP = MRC. Below this quantity, each additional unit of input generates more revenue (MRP) than cost (MRC) — hire more. Above it, MRC > MRP — hire less. For a competitive firm: MRP = w (hire until VMP = wage). For a monopsonist: MRC > w (hire where MRP = MRC, paying wage from the supply curve — yielding lower employment and lower wages than competitive equilibrium).

questions.prompt: Demand that responds strongly to price changes.
questions.explanation: Elastic demand (|PED| > 1) in factor markets matters because it affects how responsive labor demand is to wage changes. If product demand is elastic, a wage increase that raises output price will cause a large drop in product sales, sharply reducing MRP and labor demand. The elasticity of derived (factor) demand depends on: elasticity of product demand, labor's share of total cost, availability of substitute inputs, and time horizon.

questions.prompt: Goods for which demand rises when consumer income rises.
questions.explanation: Normal goods have positive income elasticity (demand rises with income). As real wages rise and worker incomes grow, demand for normal goods increases, which raises product prices and MRP, further increasing labor demand. This creates a virtuous cycle. In income distribution: rising wages expand demand for normal goods, raising employment in those industries. AP context: union wages and minimum wage effects depend on whether the product is a normal good.

questions.prompt: Demand that responds weakly to price changes.
questions.explanation: Inelastic demand for a product means labor demand in that industry is also relatively inelastic — a wage increase causes a smaller-than-proportional drop in employment. This is why unions are stronger in industries producing necessities or goods with few substitutes (e.g., healthcare workers, utility workers): the derived demand for their labor is inelastic, so wage gains come with little employment loss.

questions.prompt: Goods for which demand falls when consumer income rises.
questions.explanation: Inferior goods (negative income elasticity) see demand fall as income rises — consumers switch to higher-quality alternatives. In factor markets, rising worker incomes may shift product demand away from inferior goods, reducing MRP and labor demand in those industries. Income distribution effects: as inequality rises (fewer middle-income buyers), demand for some inferior goods rises while demand for luxury (normal) goods also rises.

questions.prompt: Decision-making by buyers as they compare prices, benefits, income limits, and opportunity costs.
questions.explanation: Consumer choice theory: consumers maximize utility given a budget constraint by choosing the bundle where MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B for all goods. Factor market link: consumer preferences determine product demand, which determines product prices, which determine MRP (MRP = MPL × P). Changes in consumer preferences therefore cascade into factor market outcomes — shifting what workers produce and how much they earn.

questions.prompt: Demand for a resource based on demand for the output it helps produce.
questions.explanation: Factor demand is derived from — driven by — demand for the final product the factor helps produce. Firms don't want labor for its own sake but for the revenue it generates (MRP). If product demand rises (higher P), MRP rises and labor demand increases. If product demand falls, MRP falls and labor demand falls. This derivation means labor market outcomes depend critically on product market conditions — a key linkage in AP FRQs.

questions.prompt: Worker skills, education, and experience.
questions.explanation: Human capital is the stock of productive knowledge, skills, health, and experience embodied in workers. Investment in human capital (education, job training, on-the-job experience) raises MPL, which shifts labor demand right and raises wages. The human capital model explains wage differentials: workers with more human capital earn more because their MRP is higher. AP FRQ: show that an education subsidy raises human capital → higher MPL → higher MRP → rightward shift of labor demand → higher wages and employment.

questions.prompt: Extra cost of hiring one more resource unit.
questions.explanation: Marginal resource cost (MRC) is the additional cost to the firm of hiring one more unit of a factor. In a competitive factor market, MRC = wage rate (w) — constant because the firm is a wage taker. In a monopsony, the firm must raise the wage to attract each additional worker, so MRC > w (MRC lies above the labor supply curve). Profit-maximizing input use: hire where MRP = MRC.

questions.prompt: Market with one buyer of labor or another resource.
questions.explanation: A monopsony is a market with a single buyer of a factor (often labor). To attract additional workers, the monopsonist must raise the wage for all workers, so MRC > w (MRC lies above the labor supply curve). Profit-maximizing hiring: set MRP = MRC; then pay the wage from the supply curve (w < MRP). Result: lower employment and lower wages than in a competitive labor market. AP FRQ: show the monopsony outcome and compare to competitive equilibrium; a minimum wage can increase both wages and employment in a monopsony.

questions.prompt: Public action, such as taxes, subsidies, regulation, or price controls, that changes market outcomes.
questions.explanation: Government intervention in factor markets includes minimum wage laws (a price floor in the labor market), union regulations, occupational licensing requirements, and anti-monopsony enforcement. A minimum wage set above the monopsony wage can increase both wages and employment simultaneously (unlike in competitive markets). AP FRQ: analyze minimum wage effects in competitive vs. monopsonistic labor markets — the outcomes differ significantly.

questions.prompt: Cost or benefit affecting third parties.
questions.explanation: An externality is a cost or benefit from a transaction that falls on third parties not part of the exchange. Negative externalities (pollution, noise) mean MSC > MPC — the market overproduces. Positive externalities (education, vaccination) mean MSB > MPB — the market underproduces. Externalities cause market failure because private decision-makers ignore social costs/benefits; government intervention (taxes, subsidies, regulation) can move output to the efficient quantity.

questions.prompt: External cost such as pollution.
questions.explanation: A negative externality imposes costs on third parties not in the transaction (pollution from a factory harms nearby residents). The market supply curve reflects only MPC, not MSC = MPC + marginal external cost (MEC). Result: market quantity (where MPC = MPB) exceeds the efficient quantity (where MSC = MSB), creating overproduction and a DWL triangle. Solutions: Pigouvian tax, quantity regulations, tradable permits (cap-and-trade).

questions.prompt: External benefit such as education or vaccination.
questions.explanation: A positive externality generates benefits for third parties not in the transaction (a vaccinated person reduces disease risk for others). The market demand curve reflects only MPB, not MSB = MPB + marginal external benefit (MEB). Result: market quantity (where MPB = MPC) is less than the efficient quantity (where MSB = MSC), creating underproduction and DWL. Solutions: subsidy to consumers or producers, government provision, or mandates.

questions.prompt: Extra cost paid by producers or consumers for one more unit, excluding external costs.
questions.explanation: Marginal private cost (MPC) is the cost borne directly by the producer for one more unit of output — the basis of the firm's supply curve. MPC ≠ MSC when negative externalities exist (MSC = MPC + MEC). Without government intervention, firms minimize their own (private) costs, ignoring external costs, leading to overproduction relative to the social optimum. A Pigouvian tax shifts MPC up to MSC, internalizing the externality.

questions.prompt: Government rules designed to reduce pollution, protect resources, or limit negative externalities.
questions.explanation: Environmental regulations (command-and-control policies) set maximum permissible levels of pollution or require specific abatement technologies. By capping emissions, they reduce market output toward the socially efficient Q*. Unlike Pigouvian taxes or cap-and-trade, regulations don't require government to measure external costs precisely — but they are less cost-efficient because they don't allow firms to trade abatement responsibilities based on relative costs.

questions.prompt: Tax designed to make producers or consumers pay the external cost of an activity.
questions.explanation: A Pigouvian (corrective) tax equals the marginal external cost at the socially optimal output level. It forces producers to internalize the externality: MSC = MPC + Pigouvian tax. The tax shifts the supply curve left by the external cost, reducing market output from the overproduced Q_market to the efficient Q*, eliminating DWL. The tax revenue can compensate victims or fund public goods. AP FRQ: set the tax equal to the vertical distance between MSC and MPC (the external cost per unit).

questions.prompt: People benefit without paying for a public good.
questions.explanation: The free rider problem arises with public goods (nonexcludable, nonrival): once the good is provided, no one can be excluded from its benefits, so individuals have an incentive to enjoy it without paying. Because everyone waits for someone else to pay, the good is underprovided or not provided at all by private markets — a market failure. Government typically provides public goods (national defense, lighthouses, basic research) funded through taxation.

questions.prompt: Good or service society believes people should consume more of, often supported through subsidies or public provision.
questions.explanation: Merit goods are goods society deems people should consume more of than they privately choose — often because of information failures, myopic decision-making, or positive externalities. Examples: healthcare, education, seat belts. Governments subsidize, provide, or mandate merit goods. Unlike public goods (nonexcludable), merit goods are often excludable and rival; the market failure is not free riding but rather underconsumption relative to social optimum.

questions.prompt: Good that is excludable but mostly nonrival until congestion occurs.
questions.explanation: A club good is excludable (non-payers can be excluded) but nonrival (one person's use doesn't reduce others' until congestion). Examples: cable TV, swimming pools, golf courses, national parks (below capacity). Because they are excludable, club goods can be provided privately by charging members. Pricing at zero (ignoring congestion) maximizes access but foregoes revenue; pricing at marginal congestion cost is efficient once congestion occurs.

questions.prompt: Good that is nonexcludable and nonrival.
questions.explanation: A public good is both nonexcludable (cannot prevent non-payers from using it) and nonrival (one person's use doesn't reduce others' use). Examples: national defense, street lighting, basic research. Because of free rider problems, private markets underprovide public goods. Government provision funded by taxation is the standard solution. Contrast: private goods (rival + excludable), club goods (nonrival + excludable), common resources (rival + nonexcludable).

questions.prompt: Situation where markets do not allocate resources efficiently.
questions.explanation: Market failure occurs when private markets fail to allocate resources efficiently — total surplus is not maximized. The four main causes in AP Micro: (1) externalities (costs/benefits not reflected in prices), (2) public goods (free rider problem), (3) imperfect information (asymmetric information, adverse selection, moral hazard), and (4) market power (monopoly, oligopoly). Each type justifies a different policy response.

questions.prompt: Spillover cost or benefit affecting people outside the market transaction.
questions.explanation: Pollution is a classic negative production externality: firms discharge pollutants that impose costs on third parties (health impacts, ecological damage). The private supply curve understates social costs (MSC = MPC + MEC), leading to Q_market > Q_efficient (overproduction). The DWL triangle shows welfare lost from excessive pollution. Remedies: Pigouvian tax (= MEC at Q*), cap-and-trade (efficient allocation of abatement), or direct regulation.

questions.prompt: Government payment that lowers costs and encourages production or consumption.
questions.explanation: A subsidy corrects underproduction from a positive externality by shifting demand rightward (consumer subsidy) or supply rightward (producer subsidy) by the marginal external benefit (MEB) per unit. Optimal subsidy = MEB at Q*. This moves market quantity from the underproduced Q_market to the socially efficient Q*, where MSB = MSC. AP FRQ: draw the D curve representing MSB above the private D (MPB) curve; show the subsidy closing the gap to achieve Q*.

questions.prompt: Market diagram showing a positive externality where social benefit exceeds private benefit.
questions.explanation: The positive externality graph shows two demand curves: D_private (MPB) below D_social (MSB = MPB + MEB). Supply (= MPC = MSC, assuming no production externality). Private equilibrium: Q_private where S = D_private (underproduction). Efficient Q*: where S = D_social. DWL triangle: between Q_private and Q*, bounded by MSB above and MPC below. A subsidy equal to MEB per unit shifts private demand up to D_social, achieving Q*.

questions.prompt: Extra benefit received by consumers for one more unit, excluding external benefits.
questions.explanation: Marginal private benefit (MPB) is the benefit received directly by the consumer — the basis of the market demand curve. When positive externalities exist, MPB understates total social benefit (MSB = MPB + MEB). Markets equilibrate at MPB = MPC, generating less output than the efficient MSB = MSC equilibrium. To bridge the gap, a subsidy of MEB per unit raises effective MPB to MSB, incentivizing consumers to demand the socially efficient quantity.

questions.prompt: Extra cost to society from one more unit, including private and external costs.
questions.explanation: Marginal social cost (MSC) = MPC (private cost) + MEC (marginal external cost). For production with negative externalities (pollution), MSC > MPC. The MSC curve is the 'true' supply curve from society's perspective. Efficient output: where MSB = MSC. Market output (where MPB = MPC) exceeds efficient Q* when MSC > MPC. A Pigouvian tax equal to MEC per unit makes the private supply curve (MPC + tax) coincide with MSC, achieving efficiency.

questions.prompt: Spillover benefit from schooling that can raise productivity, citizenship, and community well-being.
questions.explanation: Education generates positive externalities: beyond private benefits to the student (higher wages, productivity), society benefits from a more informed citizenry, lower crime rates, civic participation, and innovation spillovers. Because MSB > MPB, private markets underprovide education relative to the efficient level. Government responds with public schools, compulsory attendance laws, and subsidized student loans, moving quantity toward the MSB = MSC efficient output.

questions.prompt: Rival but nonexcludable resource.
questions.explanation: A common resource is rival (one person's use reduces others' ability to use it) but nonexcludable (no one can be prevented from using it). Examples: fisheries, groundwater, open grazing land, congested roads. Because users ignore the cost their use imposes on others, common resources are overused — the tragedy of the commons. Solutions: property rights assignment (Coase), government quotas, user fees, or community management.

questions.prompt: Extra benefit to society from one more unit, including private and external benefits.
questions.explanation: Marginal social benefit (MSB) = MPB (private benefit) + MEB (marginal external benefit). For activities with positive externalities (education, vaccination), MSB > MPB. The MSB curve is the 'true' demand curve from society's perspective. Efficient output: where MSB = MSC. Market output (where MPB = MPC) is less than efficient Q* when MSB > MPB. A Pigouvian subsidy equal to MEB per unit makes private demand (MPB + subsidy) coincide with MSB.

questions.prompt: Fixed tax that does not change behavior at the margin.
questions.explanation: A lump-sum tax is a fixed amount owed regardless of behavior (income level, consumption, or output). Because it doesn't change at the margin, it causes no behavioral distortion — no DWL — unlike an excise or income tax that affects marginal decisions. However, lump-sum taxes are regressive (take a larger share of low-income budgets) and politically difficult. AP context: lump-sum taxes are used in efficiency analysis as a baseline for comparison with distortionary taxes.

questions.prompt: Situation where one party has more information than another.
questions.explanation: Asymmetric information occurs when one party in a transaction has more or better information than the other. It creates two main market failures: (1) adverse selection — before the transaction, misinformed parties attract unfavorable counterparties (lemons market in used cars, Akerlof 1970); (2) moral hazard — after the transaction, the informed party takes riskier actions (insured parties take more risk). Government responses: mandatory disclosure, insurance mandates, warranties, licensing requirements.

questions.prompt: Market-based pollution policy that caps emissions and lets firms buy or sell permits.
questions.explanation: Tradable pollution permits (cap-and-trade) set an aggregate emissions cap, distribute permits, and allow firms to buy and sell permits. Firms with low abatement costs reduce emissions beyond their permit level and sell excess permits; high-cost firms buy permits rather than abate. This achieves the environmental target at the lowest total cost (cost-effective), unlike command-and-control standards that ignore cost differences across firms. AP context: compared to a Pigouvian tax, cap-and-trade guarantees the quantity reduction.

questions.prompt: Overuse of a shared resource.
questions.explanation: The tragedy of the commons is the overuse or depletion of a common resource because each user ignores the cost their use imposes on others. Individual rational behavior leads to collectively irrational outcomes: overfishing depletes fisheries, overgrazing destroys pastures, overuse exhausts groundwater. Solutions: (1) private property rights (internalize externalities), (2) government quotas or user fees, (3) community management agreements (Ostrom's local governance). AP FRQ: link to externalities and market failure framework.

questions.prompt: Market problem that happens when buyers or sellers lack accurate information for good decisions.
