questions.explanation: Information failure (asymmetric information) occurs when one party to a transaction has better information than the other, leading to market failure. Adverse selection: before the transaction, the uninformed party attracts unfavorable counterparties (e.g., health insurers can't distinguish healthy from unhealthy, leading to adverse risk pools). Moral hazard: after the transaction, the informed party takes more risk (e.g., insured drivers drive less carefully). Remedies: mandatory disclosure, signaling, government standards.

questions.prompt: Legal monopoly right that rewards invention by temporarily limiting who can use an innovation.
questions.explanation: A patent grants an inventor a temporary legal monopoly (20 years under U.S. law) over the use of a new invention. This exclusive right allows the inventor to charge P > MC and earn economic profit, incentivizing R&D investment. Cost: monopoly pricing creates DWL during the patent period. Benefit: innovation and knowledge spillovers after expiration. AP context: patents are a legal barrier to entry — a policy trade-off between dynamic efficiency (innovation) and static efficiency (competitive pricing).

questions.prompt: Additional output from one more input.
questions.explanation: Marginal product (most often MPL) = ΔQ/ΔL — the additional output from one more variable input unit. Initially rising (increasing returns as workers specialize), MPL eventually falls (diminishing marginal returns as workers share fixed capital). The falling MPL directly causes MC to rise: MC = w/MPL. MRP = MPL × MR is the firm's factor demand curve. AP FRQ: compute MPL from a table and use it to find MC and the profit-maximizing employment level.

questions.prompt: Direct relationship between price and quantity supplied.
questions.explanation: The law of supply: ceteris paribus, as price rises, quantity supplied increases (direct relationship) — producing an upward-sloping supply curve. Producers are motivated to supply more at higher prices because higher prices cover higher marginal costs (rising MC due to diminishing marginal returns). AP FRQ distinction: a change in price = movement along supply; a change in a non-price determinant (input costs, technology, taxes) = shift of the supply curve.

questions.prompt: Comparing additional benefits and additional costs.
questions.explanation: Marginal analysis compares the additional benefit and additional cost of one more unit of an action. Optimal decision: pursue any action where MB ≥ MC; stop when MB = MC; avoid if MB < MC. This principle runs through all of AP Micro: output choice (MR = MC), input hiring (MRP = MRC), consumer choice (MU/P equalized), pollution control (MSB = MSC), and taxation analysis (compare DWL at the margin). Marginal thinking is the organizing framework of microeconomics.

questions.prompt: Inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded.
questions.explanation: The law of demand: ceteris paribus, as price rises, quantity demanded falls (inverse relationship). This produces a downward-sloping demand curve explained by two effects: the substitution effect (good becomes relatively more expensive) and the income effect (higher price lowers real purchasing power). AP FRQ distinction: a change in price = movement along the demand curve; a change in a non-price determinant = shift of the entire demand curve.

questions.prompt: Lower opportunity cost that explains specialization and trade gains.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is the basis for mutually beneficial trade: produce the good for which you have the lower opportunity cost, even if you are absolutely less efficient at everything. Specializing according to comparative advantage and trading allows both parties to consume beyond their own PPC. AP cumulative: comparative advantage (Unit 1) connects to consumer surplus gains from trade (Unit 2) and efficiency concepts (Unit 3). Always calculate opportunity costs from the given data to determine who specializes.

questions.prompt: Next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative given up when a decision is made — it encompasses both explicit (cash) and implicit (forgone earnings) costs. On the PPC, opportunity cost = the slope (units of Y sacrificed per unit of X gained). In firm cost analysis, economic profit subtracts opportunity costs; a firm earning zero economic profit is covering all opportunity costs (normal profit). AP FRQ trap: always include implicit costs when calculating economic profit.

questions.prompt: Relationship between inputs and output.
questions.explanation: The production function Q = f(L, K) maps input combinations (labor, capital) to maximum feasible output. Short run: capital is fixed, labor varies (total product, AP, MP schedule). Long run: all inputs variable (isoquant-isocost analysis, LRATC). The shape of the production function — increasing then decreasing returns to the variable input — directly generates the U-shaped cost curves central to AP Micro firm analysis.

questions.prompt: Charging different prices to different consumers for the same product.
questions.explanation: Price discrimination means charging different prices to different consumers for the same good based on willingness to pay, not cost differences. Requires: market power, ability to segment consumers, and prevention of resale. 1st-degree (perfect): charges each consumer their reservation price — captures all CS, achieves allocatively efficient Q (like competition). 3rd-degree: segment by group (student, senior, peak/off-peak). AP FRQ: 1st-degree discrimination eliminates DWL but transfers all CS to the monopolist as profit.

questions.prompt: Cost that stays unchanged as output rises or falls.
questions.explanation: Fixed costs don't change as output rises or falls in the short run (rent, loan payments, salaried staff). AFC = FC/Q continuously declines. Crucially, fixed costs do not affect MC (MC = ΔTVC/ΔQ) and therefore don't affect the profit-maximizing output choice (MR = MC). However, fixed costs determine whether a firm earns profit or loss: if TR > TVC (P > AVC), the firm operates; it exits only if losses persist in the long run (P < ATC).

questions.prompt: Model showing scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, and trade-offs.
questions.explanation: The PPC shows the maximum output combinations of two goods with given resources and technology. Points on the curve: efficient. Inside: inefficient. Outside: currently unattainable. Outward shifts = economic growth (more resources or better technology). Slope = opportunity cost; concave shape = law of increasing opportunity cost. AP cumulative connection: the PPC underpins comparative advantage, specialization, gains from trade, and basic scarcity concepts tested across all units.

questions.prompt: Condition where wants exceed available resources.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the foundation of economics: unlimited human wants outstrip finite resources (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship). Every economic decision involves trade-offs — choosing more of one thing means less of another. Scarcity creates opportunity costs, drives specialization and trade, and makes the PPC a relevant model. Without scarcity, there is no need for economic decision-making or markets.

questions.prompt: Declining extra output from additional variable inputs.
questions.explanation: The law of diminishing marginal returns (short run): as more units of a variable input are added to fixed inputs, the marginal product eventually decreases. This occurs because extra workers must share fixed capital. Diminishing returns → rising MC → upward-sloping supply curve. AP cumulative connection: diminishing returns underlies cost curves (MC, AVC, ATC), the shutdown decision, long-run vs. short-run production, and factor market analysis (falling MRP).

questions.prompt: Market where one firm can supply output at lower cost than many firms.
questions.explanation: A natural monopoly exists when economies of scale are so pervasive that LRATC falls over the entire relevant market output range — one large firm can serve the market at lower per-unit cost than multiple competing firms. Examples: electricity grids, water supply, broadband networks. Government regulation options: marginal-cost pricing (P = MC, allocatively efficient but requires a subsidy), average-cost pricing (P = ATC, breaks even), or price caps.

questions.prompt: Market with many firms selling differentiated products.
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition: many sellers, differentiated products (market power), low barriers to entry/exit. Short run: earns economic profit or loss. Long run: entry/exit drives economic profit to zero (demand tangent to ATC). Long-run outcome: P > MC (allocative inefficiency, DWL) and P > min ATC (productive inefficiency, excess capacity). AP cumulative: compare to perfect competition (P = min ATC = MC, both efficient) and monopoly (higher profit, less output).

questions.prompt: Extra revenue created by hiring one more resource unit.
questions.explanation: MRP = MPL × MR measures the additional revenue from hiring one more unit of a factor. In competitive output markets, MRP = MPL × P (= VMP). MRP is the firm's factor demand curve: hire until MRP = MRC (= wage in competitive labor markets). MRP shifts when: product price changes (demand shifts), technology changes (MPL changes), or capital changes. AP cumulative: MRP bridges production theory (Unit 3), factor markets (Unit 5), and market structure (Unit 4).

questions.prompt: Non-price factor changing demand.
questions.explanation: Demand shifters are non-price factors moving the entire demand curve (Tastes, Related-good prices, Income, Buyer count, Expectations — TRIBE). In factor markets, labor demand shifts when: the output product price changes (raises/lowers MRP), technology changes (changes MPL), or the number of hiring firms changes. AP cumulative: demand shifters connect Unit 2 (product market) to Unit 5 (factor market) — product demand changes cascade into factor market outcomes.

questions.prompt: Demand for a resource based on demand for the output it helps produce.
questions.explanation: Factor demand is derived from — and driven by — consumer demand for the final product. Firms hire workers not for labor itself but for the revenue labor generates (MRP = MPL × MR). A rise in product demand raises the product price, raising MRP, which shifts labor demand right and raises wages. Derived demand connects product markets to factor markets — a central AP Micro linkage tested in multi-part FRQs.

questions.prompt: Market for resources such as labor, land, and capital.
questions.explanation: Factor markets are where firms buy inputs (labor, land, capital, entrepreneurship) from households, completing the circular flow. Factor prices (wages, rent, interest, profit) are set by factor supply and factor demand (= MRP). Factor markets link product markets (through derived demand) to income distribution (wages = workers' income). AP cumulative: factor market FRQs connect production theory (MPL), output market structure (MR), and labor market intervention (minimum wage, monopsony).

questions.prompt: Non-price factor changing supply.
questions.explanation: Supply shifters are non-price factors moving the entire supply curve (input Prices, Resource availability, Innovation/technology, Alternative goods in production, Taxes and subsidies, Expectations, Sellers count — PIRATES). In labor markets, supply shifts when: immigration changes labor force size, non-wage job conditions change, education completion rates change, or wages in alternative occupations change. AP cumulative: supply shifters in product markets and factor markets appear in multi-part FRQ chains.

questions.prompt: Extra cost of hiring one more resource unit.
questions.explanation: MRC is the additional cost of hiring one more input unit. Competitive labor market: MRC = wage (constant, firm is a wage taker). Monopsony: to attract additional workers the firm must raise the wage for all workers, so MRC > w (MRC lies above the labor supply curve). Profit-maximizing rule: hire where MRP = MRC. AP cumulative: comparing competitive (MRC = w) to monopsonistic (MRC > w) labor markets is a high-priority FRQ topic.

questions.prompt: Market with one seller and barriers to entry.
questions.explanation: A monopoly is the single seller of a good with no close substitutes, protected by high barriers to entry. The monopolist chooses Q where MR = MC and charges P from the demand curve (P > MC). Outcomes: economic profit, lower output than competitive equilibrium, higher price, DWL (allocative inefficiency), and productive inefficiency (unless output happens to be at min ATC). AP cumulative: monopoly is the reference for understanding market failure, antitrust, and natural monopoly regulation.

questions.prompt: Situation where markets do not allocate resources efficiently.
questions.explanation: Market failure is any situation where private markets fail to maximize total surplus. Four AP Micro causes: (1) externalities (MSC ≠ MPC or MSB ≠ MPB), (2) public goods (nonexcludable/nonrival → free rider problem), (3) asymmetric information (adverse selection, moral hazard), (4) market power (monopoly/oligopoly restricting output below Q*). Each cause justifies a different government response (Pigouvian tax, public provision, disclosure rules, antitrust). AP cumulative: link market failures across Units 4 and 6.

questions.prompt: External benefit such as education or vaccination.
questions.explanation: A positive externality benefits third parties not in the transaction (vaccination reduces disease risk for others; education raises community productivity). Because MSB > MPB, private markets underproduce at Q_private (where MPB = MPC < MSB). DWL: the triangle between Q_private and Q* bounded by MSB above and MPC. Correction: subsidy of MEB per unit to producers or consumers, government provision, or mandates. AP cumulative: connects Units 2 (D/S), 3 (efficiency), and 6 (government role).

questions.prompt: Cost or benefit affecting third parties.
questions.explanation: An externality is a cost or benefit of a transaction borne by third parties not part of the exchange. Negative externalities → overproduction (MSC > MPC; market ignores external cost). Positive externalities → underproduction (MSB > MPB; market ignores external benefit). In both cases, market quantity ≠ socially efficient quantity, generating DWL. Corrective policies: Pigouvian tax (negative externality), subsidy (positive externality), regulations, property rights (Coase theorem).

questions.prompt: External cost such as pollution.
questions.explanation: A negative externality imposes costs on uninvolved third parties (factory pollution harms neighbors). Private supply (MPC) understates full social cost (MSC = MPC + MEC), so market output Q_market > efficient Q* (where MSC = MSB). The DWL triangle lies between Q* and Q_market. Corrective policies: Pigouvian tax (= MEC per unit at Q*), cap-and-trade, direct quantity limits. AP cumulative: negative externalities connect cost analysis (Unit 3), market failure (Unit 6), and government intervention tools.
